mnemonics.title: CP15 Register Organization
mnemonics.content: Think of CP15 as a library with books (registers) organized by shelf (CRn) and section (CRm). Use: MRC p15, 0, Rd, c1, c0, 0 - Read from c1 shelf, section 0. Format: opcode1, Rd, CRn, CRm, opcode2

mnemonics.title: Cache Write Policies
mnemonics.content: Write-Through = Write to BOTH (cache + memory). Write-Back = Write to BACK (cache only, write back later). Remember: TWIN = Through-Write-IN, BACK = Back-logged

mnemonics.title: TLB Purpose
mnemonics.content: TLB = Translation Lookaside Buffer. Think of it as a dictionary for addresses: Look up virtual word → Find physical meaning. Avoids walking through page tables (the long dictionary).

acronyms.usage: System control coprocessor in ARM providing MMU and cache control interface
Hardware component that translates virtual addresses to physical addresses using page tables
Special cache storing recent virtual-to-physical address translations
High-speed cache storing frequently accessed program instructions
High-speed cache storing frequently accessed data values
ARM instruction to write data from CPU register to coprocessor
ARM instruction to read data from coprocessor to CPU register
Main control register in ARM for enabling MMU, caches, and other features

visualTips.visualization: Draw an inverted pyramid: Top = CPU (smallest, fastest), Middle = L1 cache, Bottom = L2/L3 cache (larger, slower), Bottom = Main Memory (largest, slowest). Label capacities: L1 ~16-64KB, L2 ~256KB-2MB, RAM ~GBs.
Visualize as MRC/MCR instruction: |opcode1(3)|Rd(4)|CRn(4)|CRm(4)|opcode2(3)|. Each field selects specific register like c1 for control, c7 for cache operations.
Draw two boxes: Virtual Address →[Page Table in Memory]→ Physical Address. Show TLB as a small shortcut box bypassing the page table with ✓ or ✗ (hit/miss).
Draw two flowcharts: Write-Through = CPU→Cache→Memory (both updated). Write-Back = CPU→Cache (marked dirty)→Later when evicted→Memory.

analogies.analogy: CP15 is like the building manager's office - controls access to all facilities (MMU), security (caches), and configuration (system settings).
Cache is like a secretary's desk drawer - frequently used documents kept close for quick access rather than searching through filing cabinets (main memory).
TLB is like a frequently-called phone contacts list - instead of looking up every number in the directory, quick access to recent/favorite contacts saves time.
Virtual memory is like a library's catalog system - gives illusion of having access to all books even if only a few are physically on the desk at once.
Write buffer is like a collection tray at a copy machine - drop documents to be copied and continue working without waiting for each copy to finish.
